sections.title: Genitive Case Endings
sections.explanation: The genitive case is formed by changing the ending of a noun and its article. Masculine nouns: -ος → -ου (ο φίλος → του φίλου), -ης → -η (ο ναύτης → του ναύτη), -ας → -α (ο μπαμπάς → του μπαμπά). Feminine nouns: -α or -η → -ας or -ης (η μητέρα → της μητέρας, η θάλασσα → της θάλασσας, η πόλη → της πόλης). Neuter nouns: -ο → -ου (το βιβλίο → του βιβλίου), -ι → -ιού or remains the same (το παιδί → του παιδιού). The definite article changes as follows: singular ο → του, η → της, το → του; plural οι → των, τα → των.
sections.tip: For possession, swap the nominative article for the genitive: ο/το → του, η → της. Plural of all genders uses 'των'.

examples.en: the friend (nom.) → of the friend (gen., masculine -ος → -ου)
the sailor (nom.) → of the sailor (gen., masculine -ης → -η)
the dad (nom.) → of the dad (gen., masculine -ας → -α)
the mother (nom.) → of the mother (gen., feminine -α → -ας)
the sea (nom.) → of the sea (gen., feminine -α → -ας)
the child (nom.) → of the child (gen., neuter -ί → -ιού)

exercises.prompt: What is the genitive form of 'ο φίλος' (the friend)?
exercises.explanation: Masculine -ος nouns change to -ου in the genitive, and the article ο becomes του → του φίλου.

exercises.prompt: What is the genitive form of 'η μητέρα' (the mother)?
exercises.explanation: Feminine -α nouns change to -ας in the genitive, and the article η becomes της → της μητέρας.

exercises.prompt: What is the genitive article for ALL plural nouns (all genders)?
exercises.explanation: In the plural, the genitive article is 'των' for all three genders: των φίλων, των μητέρων, των παιδιών.

sections.title: Uses of the Genitive
sections.explanation: The genitive case has several important uses in Greek. 1) Possession: it shows that something belongs to someone or something (η τσάντα της Μαρίας = Maria's bag; το αυτοκίνητο του Γιώργη = George's car). 2) After certain prepositions: εκτός (except/outside of) and λόγω (due to) govern the genitive. Note: από (from/of) takes the accusative case. 3) Fixed phrases and compound words: καλημέρα comes from καλή + μέρα (good + day), καληνύχτα from καλή + νύχτα. 4) Expressions of time and price: της εβδομάδας (per week/weekly), του χρόνου (next year, literally 'of the year').
sections.tip: Think of the genitive as answering the question 'whose?' or 'of what/whom?' — it links two nouns together.

examples.en: Maria's bag (possession — the bag of Maria)
George's car (possession — the car of George)
Next year we will go. (time expression using genitive)

exercises.prompt: What does 'η τσάντα της Μαρίας' mean?
exercises.explanation: The genitive 'της Μαρίας' expresses possession — the bag belonging to Maria.

exercises.prompt: Which preposition commonly takes a genitive noun in Greek?
exercises.explanation: 'Λόγω' (due to) governs the genitive case, e.g. λόγω της βροχής (due to the rain).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'George's car' in Greek?
exercises.explanation: Possession is expressed with the genitive: του Γιώργη (of George) placed after the noun.

sections.title: Genitive of Personal Pronouns
sections.explanation: Greek personal pronouns have weak (clitic) genitive forms used to express possession. These are: μου (my/mine), σου (your/yours — singular), του (his/its for masculine), της (her/its for feminine), μας (our/ours), σας (your/yours — plural or formal), τους (their/theirs). These weak forms are placed after the noun they modify, unlike in English where the possessive comes before. For example: το σπίτι μου (my house), ο φίλος σου (your friend), η μητέρα του (his mother), το παιδί της (her child), το σπίτι μας (our house), ο δρόμος σας (your road), το αυτοκίνητο τους (their car).
sections.tip: Weak genitive pronouns always follow the noun: 'το σπίτι μου' (lit. 'the house my') — never before it like in English.

examples.en: your friend (lit. the friend of-you, singular)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'my house' in Greek?
exercises.explanation: Weak genitive pronouns follow the noun: το σπίτι μου (the house my = my house).
'Σας' is the genitive pronoun for 'you' in the plural or formal register → your friend (plural/formal).

exercises.prompt: Which weak genitive pronoun means 'her' (belonging to a female)?
exercises.explanation: 'Της' is the weak genitive form for 'her' — e.g. η τσάντα της (her bag). 'Του' is his, 'τους' is their.
